ACTIVITIES:

Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman LLP, founded in 1868, is a global law firm headquartered in New York City. With over 700 lawyers across 20 offices, it serves clients in energy, technology, and finance, specializing in M&A, regulatory, and litigation. Generating over $700 million annually, Pillsbury advised on 2024 renewable energy transactions, recognized by Columbia Law School. Its culture emphasizes innovation, diversity, and pro bono work, with strong associate training programs. Competing with Baker McKenzie, Pillsbury stands out for its energy and tech regulatory expertise. In 2024, it expanded its AI and ESG practices, focusing on sustainable finance. Recent initiatives include cloud-based legal tech and partnerships with nonprofits like Lawyers Alliance for New York, reinforcing its community impact.

+ PERKINS COIE [3 News That Matters]

+ ASHURST PERKINS COIE MERGER GAINS STRONG SUPPORT FROM PARTNERS

The merger of global law firms Ashurst and Perkins Coie into the entity Ashurst Perkins Coie has received overwhelming support from the partnerships of both firms. This decision was finalized following a consultation period, with the approval announced on 13th April 2026. The merger is expected to close in the third quarter of the year, contingent upon customary closing conditions.Mark Birnbaum, executive committee chair of Perkins Coie, emphasized the significance of this approval, stating that it reflects the partners' commitment to creating a forward-looking firm. He noted that the merger underlines a shared confidence in their capacity to grow and deliver exceptional legal services in an evolving global market. Paul Jenkins, CEO of Ashurst, also highlighted the alignment between the two firms, asserting that their combined strengths will benefit their clients significantly.The merged firm aims to focus on key sectors including technology, energy, infrastructure, and financial services. Bill Malley, firmwide managing partner at Perkins Coie, expressed optimism that the combination would enhance the firms' abilities to meet clients’ complex cross-border legal needs. The new entity is set to boast approximately 3,000 lawyers across 52 offices in 32 countries, with major hubs located in Seattle, London, Sydney, and New York.The merger, first announced last year, brings together two firms with combined revenues of around USD 2.8 billion. Their lawyers have extensive experience in international markets, having worked in cities such as Brussels, Dubai, Frankfurt, Hong Kong, Paris, Seoul, Shenzhen, Singapore, and Tokyo. The leadership of the new firm will be shared by Malley and Jenkins as global co-CEOs, while Ashurst's global chair, Karen Davies, and Perkins Coie's corporate practice chair, Brian Eiting, will serve as co-chairs.As the legal landscape continues to evolve, the Ashurst Perkins Coie combination is poised to enhance its competitive edge by leveraging the strengths of both firms. This strategic move not only positions the new entity to better serve its clients but also sets the stage for sustained growth and performance in the legal industry.

+ PERKINS COIE AND ASHURST UNITE TO FORM ASHURST PERKINS COIE

NEW YORK — Perkins Coie and Ashurst announced today that the partnerships of both firms have voted overwhelmingly to approve their proposed combination. The approval follows consultation from both firms and reflects strong support for the combination of two law firms with highly complementary geographic reach, sector depth, and practice strength. The date of this announcement is recorded as 2026-04-14 16:18:02.950901.Upon closing, Ashurst Perkins Coie will emerge as a globally integrated firm with combined revenues of approximately US$2.8 billion. The new entity will boast around 3,000 lawyers across more than 50 offices, including flagship hubs in Seattle, London, Sydney, and New York. Ashurst Perkins Coie will be particularly noted for its strengths in the technology, energy and infrastructure, and financial services sectors—industries that are pivotal to the ongoing global economic transformation.Bill Malley, the Firmwide Managing Partner at Perkins Coie, remarked on the significance of this merger by stating, 'Our partnership's approval reflects a shared belief that combining to form Ashurst Perkins Coie will create a truly differentiated global legal platform—one with the scale, sector depth, and technological leadership to meet our clients' increasingly complex, cross-border needs.' This sentiment underscores the strategic vision behind the combination.Paul Jenkins, Global CEO of Ashurst, echoed Malley’s sentiments, emphasizing the strong alignment between the two firms. 'This vote confirms the strong alignment between our firms and our joint ambition for the future,' he stated, highlighting the complementary expertise across sectors and practice areas that will benefit clients. Jenkins added that the merger will create a compelling platform for top legal talent.The combination remains subject to customary closing conditions and is expected to finalize in the third quarter of 2026. As the legal landscape becomes more complex, Ashurst Perkins Coie aims to position itself as a trusted advisor, ready to navigate the intricacies of the global market and deliver exceptional legal services.

+ PERKINS COIE REENTERS CHINA MARKET WITH NEW SHENZHEN OFFICE

Perkins Coie has officially opened a representative office in Shenzhen, marking the firm's significant comeback in the Chinese market. This development comes on the heels of a strategic decision to re-establish an on-the-ground presence in the region, with the office inaugurated on 14th April 2026.The Seattle-based law firm had previously operated offices in Beijing and Shanghai, which were closed in 2024. At that time, Perkins Coie indicated plans to pivot towards Shenzhen, citing the city’s rapid growth as a key tech and manufacturing hub. The firm aims to leverage this new office to enhance its support for Asia-based companies navigating US and cross-border legal needs.Leading the Shenzhen office will be Bing Ai, serving as chief representative and managing partner, along with Wei Yuan, who will act as a representative and litigation partner. Both lawyers are US-trained and bring extensive experience in advising technology-focused clients. Ai specializes in patent procurement and portfolio management in sectors such as semiconductors and medical devices, while Yuan is adept at handling patent infringement disputes.Bill Malley, Perkins Coie’s managing partner, emphasized the firm's commitment to providing US legal expertise across borders. He noted that the opening of the Shenzhen office reflects a dedication to assisting clients in navigating the complexities of global markets, especially in light of the increasing challenges they face.As Perkins Coie re-establishes its presence in mainland China, it stands apart from the trend of office closures and headcount reductions by many leading US firms due to geopolitical tensions and regulatory challenges. However, several international firms have recently opened offices in Shenzhen, attracted by its status within the Greater Bay Area, which has seen substantial economic growth. Perkins Coie’s strategic move comes as the firm prepares for a potential merger with UK-based Ashurst, which would create a top 20 global firm with projected annual revenues of around $2.7 billion.

+ COOLEY LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ CROSBY SECURES $60M IN FUNDING, BACKED BY COOLEY LLP

Contract review start-up Crosby has successfully raised $60 million in its latest funding round, marking its third capital raise in less than a year. The funding announcement was made on 3rd April 2026, highlighting the firm’s rapid growth and the interest from prominent investors, including the West Coast-based law firm Cooley LLP.The Series B round was co-led by Lux Capital and Index Ventures, with participation from renowned venture capital firms such as Sequoia, 01 Advisors, and Bain Capital Ventures, as well as angel investor Elad Gil. With this new influx of capital, Crosby plans to enhance its technology capabilities and provide better support to its growing customer base, which includes notable software development firms Clay and Rogo, as well as the financial services operations platform Ramp.Crosby combines artificial intelligence with legal expertise to expedite the contract review process. The firm reported a significant increase in the value of its negotiated contracts, which has soared to just over $1 billion. This is a remarkable rise from the $30 million in brokered contracts when Crosby emerged from stealth mode last year, showcasing the firm’s impressive trajectory in the legal tech space.In a recent blog post, Crosby emphasized its commitment to innovation, stating, 'As we’ve scaled, we’ve also started experimenting with entirely new technology experiences.' The firm has introduced various features, including simulations of counterparty responses to contract redlines, voice agents that negotiate on behalf of clients, and a platform that allows clients to oversee and provide input on Crosby’s work.Founded in 2024 by Ryan Daniels and John Sarihan, Crosby has quickly established itself in the legal tech landscape. Daniels, a former associate at Cooley, has an extensive background in tech entrepreneurship, while Sarihan brings experience as a former Google software engineer and tech lead for Crosby client Ramp. Together, they are steering Crosby towards a future of enhanced contract negotiation and review, supported by substantial investments from Cooley and other leading firms.

As Cooley LLP continues to expand its litigation department, the firm is poised to leverage its expertise in addressing complex legal challenges while maintaining a strong commitment to its core values.The growth of Cooley LLP's litigation practice is particularly noteworthy as it reflects broader trends within the industry, where firms are increasingly focusing on specialized legal services. + SIDLEY AUSTIN LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ SIDLEY AUSTIN FACILITATES USD2.1BN COLLABORATION FOR TELIX PHARMACEUTICALS

Sidley Austin has advised Australia-based biopharmaceutical company Telix Pharmaceuticals on its strategic collaboration with American biotechnology firm Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, under an agreement valued at up to USD2.1 billion. The announcement comes on the heels of a robust expansion strategy by Telix, aiming to enhance its footprint in the radiopharmaceutical space.On 21st April 2026, it was disclosed that Telix will co-develop and co-commercialise next-generation radiopharmaceutical therapies with Regeneron through a 50:50 cost and profit-sharing model. This innovative approach underscores the commitment of both companies to advance therapeutic options in the oncology field, leveraging each other's expertise and resources.As part of the collaboration, Telix Pharmaceuticals will receive an upfront payment of USD40 million for four initial therapeutic programmes. The potential to earn up to USD2.1 billion over the duration of the partnership reflects the high stakes and anticipated impact of this alliance on the industry. Joshua Hofheimer, a partner at Sidley Austin, highlighted the complexities involved in such collaborations, stating, 'These considerations affect governance, regulatory matters, and intellectual property, in particular.'The strategic partnership is expected to pave the way for significant advancements in the development of radiopharmaceuticals, a sector that is gaining traction in cancer treatment. With the combined capabilities of Telix Pharmaceuticals and Regeneron, the collaboration aims to deliver innovative therapies that could transform patient care.As mergers and acquisitions (M&A) activities continue to rise across the region, legal firms like Sidley Austin are playing a crucial role in navigating the complexities of these deals. The experience showcased in advising Telix Pharmaceuticals may further solidify Sidley’s reputation as a leading advisor in the biopharmaceutical sector, especially as regulatory landscapes evolve.

+ CFTC ENGAGES SIDLEY AUSTIN AND LATHAM FOR INNOVATIVE TASK FORCE

The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) has enlisted the expertise of attorneys from SidleyAustin and Latham & Watkins to form an Innovation Task Force aimed at enhancing the regulatory framework surrounding the derivatives market. This initiative marks a significant step towards embracing technological advancements and fostering innovation within the financial sector.On 11th April 2026, the CFTC announced the formation of the task force, recognizing the need to address the rapidly evolving landscape of digital assets and trading technologies. The involvement of Sidley Austin, known for its robust practice in financial services and regulatory compliance, underscores the commission's commitment to developing informed, forward-thinking policies that reflect current market realities.Sidley Austin's attorneys bring a wealth of experience in navigating complex regulatory environments, making them a valuable asset to the CFTC's mission. Their engagement is expected to provide critical insights and guidance on best practices, particularly as the derivatives market continues to integrate innovative technologies such as blockchain and artificial intelligence.The Innovation Task Force is part of a broader strategy by the CFTC to not only regulate but also to facilitate innovation in the derivatives space. With the support of firms like Sidley Austin, the commission aims to strike a balance between effective oversight and the encouragement of technological advancement, ensuring that the U.S. remains a leader in global financial markets.As the task force convenes, industry stakeholders will be closely monitoring developments, particularly regarding potential regulatory changes that may arise from the collaboration between the CFTC and Sidley Austin. This partnership could pave the way for new policies that better accommodate the needs of market participants while safeguarding the integrity of the financial system.

+ MORRISON & FOERSTER EXAMINES GLOBAL PE TRENDS AS 2026 APPROACHES

As geopolitical unpredictability continues around the world, the outlook for the global private equity (PE) market in 2026 is under scrutiny. In its sixth annual Global PE Trends and Outlook report, Morrison & Foerster provides a comprehensive review of key PE trends from 2025 while offering predictions from global, regional, and sector perspectives for the year ahead. This analysis, released on 19th January 2026, delves into jurisdictions including the U.S., Latin America, Europe, the UK, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, and India.Morrison & Foerster's report highlights a variety of industry sectors that are set to shape the PE landscape in 2026. Key themes discussed include Artificial Intelligence (AI), Advanced Digital Goods (ADG), Data Centers, Finance, Food & Agriculture, Life Sciences, Professional Services, Special Situations, and Sustainability & Corporate Responsibility. These sectors are anticipated to drive investment decisions and influence market dynamics as private equity firms adapt to an ever-evolving global environment.The insights provided by Morrison & Foerster come at a time when economic uncertainties and changing regulatory frameworks are impacting investment strategies. The firm emphasizes the importance of understanding regional nuances and sector-specific trends that investors must navigate to capitalize on emerging opportunities. With the global private equity market poised for transformation, stakeholders are advised to stay informed on these developments.Moreover, Morrison & Foerster’s report underscores the significance of sustainability and corporate responsibility in shaping investment criteria. As global focus shifts towards sustainable practices, private equity firms are increasingly evaluating how environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors influence investment decisions. This trend is expected to gain momentum in 2026, prompting firms to integrate these considerations into their strategic frameworks.In conclusion, Morrison & Foerster's Global PE Trends and Outlook for 2026 presents a detailed examination of the private equity landscape, reflecting on past trends while projecting future possibilities. As investors prepare for the challenges and opportunities that lie ahead, the insights offered in this report serve as a valuable resource, equipping them with the knowledge necessary to make informed decisions in the evolving market context.

+ GREENBERG TRAURIG, LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ GREENBERG TRAURIG'S ROLE IN TRANSITION OF GOLDEN ENTERTAINMENT TO PRIVATE OWNERSHIP

Global law firm Greenberg Traurig, LLP represented Blake L. Sartini in the successful transition of Golden Entertainment to a private, family-owned operation. The transaction was finalized on 30th April 2026, marking a significant milestone for Sartini, who has been an influential figure in the gaming industry.As part of the deal, Golden Entertainment shareholders received a cash dividend of $2.75 and 0.902 shares of VICI Properties Inc. (NYSE:VICI) for each share of Golden stock they held. This arrangement is expected to provide shareholders with immediate liquidity while maintaining their investment in a major player in the gaming and hospitality sector.In the transaction, VICI Properties acquired the real estate assets associated with seven of Golden Entertainment's casino resorts. A triple-net master lease was established with entities controlled by Mr. Sartini, allowing Golden Entertainment to continue operating its full portfolio of casinos and taverns without interruption.Michael J. Bonner, Co-Managing Shareholder of Greenberg Traurig's Las Vegas office, expressed pride in representing Sartini, stating, "We were honored to represent Blake L. Sartini in returning Golden Entertainment to private family ownership. We congratulate Blake and the entire Golden Entertainment team!" This deal underscores Greenberg Traurig’s extensive expertise in corporate transactions and real estate law.The Greenberg Traurig team that facilitated this complex transaction included notable figures such as Dmitriy A. Tartakovskiy, Jim Mace, and James Doyle, among others. With approximately 3,100 lawyers across 51 locations globally, Greenberg Traurig continues to be recognized for its innovative legal services and strategic solutions, further solidifying its position as a leader in the legal industry.

+ GREENBERG TRAURIG TOKYO DISCUSSES FUTURE TRENDS IN REAL ASSETS

Greenberg Traurig, LLP's Tokyo office recently hosted a significant briefing for the Asian Association for Investors in Non-Listed Real Estate Vehicles (ANREV), gathering institutional investors and industry experts to delve into the evolving landscape of real assets, data centres, and artificial intelligence. This event took place on the 30th of April, 2026, and featured a panel discussion entitled 'Real Assets & New Technologies: Data Centres & AI Opportunities.'The panel included Greenberg Traurig Shareholder Joel H. Rothstein, who chairs the firm's Asia Real Estate Practice. Rothstein and his fellow panelists engaged in discussions about the transformative impact of AI on capital allocation, infrastructure planning, and investment strategies within the global real asset markets. A key point of focus was the surging demand for data centre assets across the Asia-Pacific region, driven by the rapid digitalization of economies.Rothstein emphasized Greenberg Traurig's ongoing commitment to fostering dialogue around pivotal industry trends shaping the global real estate and infrastructure sectors. He stated, 'These conversations are increasingly important as investors and developers navigate the rapid convergence of digital infrastructure, real estate, and emerging technologies.' This initiative reflects the firm’s strategic alignment with trends that are becoming essential for stakeholders in the real assets market.With a practice that centers on cross-border real estate transactions and investments, Rothstein advises a diverse array of clients, including institutional investors, private equity sponsors, and asset managers. His expertise extends to acquisitions, dispositions, joint ventures, and development projects spanning Asia, the United States, and other international markets. By leveraging Greenberg Traurig's global platform, he is well-positioned to support complex, multijurisdictional investment strategies that incorporate digital infrastructure and alternative real assets.Greenberg Traurig, LLP, with approximately 3,100 lawyers across 51 locations worldwide, is recognized for its exceptional legal services and innovative approach to real estate and infrastructure law. The firm's commitment to excellence has earned it accolades as a 2025 BTI 'Best of the Best Recommended Law Firm,' alongside consistent rankings in prestigious lists such as the Am Law Global 100 and Law360 400. This event signifies Greenberg Traurig's proactive role in addressing the intersection of real estate and technology, crucial for investors navigating today's dynamic market.

+ GREENBERG TRAURIG'S MELTONES ROCK FOR A CAUSE AT LAW ROCKS LA

Greenberg Traurig, LLP's house band, The Meltones, clinched victory in the Law Rocks Los Angeles battle of the bands during a sold-out concert at the iconic Whisky A Go Go in West Hollywood. On 29th April 2026, the event showcased the band's energetic performance and philanthropic spirit, raising over $65,000 through donations and sponsorships.Of the funds raised, $41,000 will directly support The Headstrong Project, a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing mental health resources to military veterans. The Law Rocks event, which took place over three nights, set a record by accumulating more than $569,000, marking the highest total raised in Los Angeles in the past 15 years.The Meltones, known for their vibrant performances, opened the show with a Green Day anthem, igniting enthusiasm among the audience. This appearance marked the band's second Los Angeles gig, and their success in the competition was determined through a combination of donations, ticket sales, and judges' scores. Greenberg Traurig also served as a Hall of Fame sponsor for the event, underscoring the firm's commitment to community engagement.Featuring a lineup of talented musicians, The Meltones consist of Global Real Estate Co-Chair Stephen L. Rabinowitz and Miami Real Estate Shareholder Burt Bruton on guitar, Albany Government Law & Policy Shareholder Christopher A. Cernik on keyboard, and vocalists including Las Vegas and San Francisco Construction Law Shareholder Michael R. Hogue, New York Litigation Of Counsel Ashley A. LeBlanc, and Chief Shareholder Recruiting and Integration Officer Tracy Newirth. Guest artists Tom Helf on drums and Daniel Blumberg on bass rounded out the band, adding to the dynamic performance.The Meltones, named in honor of the firm’s co-founder Mel Greenberg, have been an integral part of Greenberg Traurig's culture since the mid-1980s. Their continued success, including a recent victory at Law Rocks NYC where they raised over $100,000 for The Door, highlights the firm's dedication to philanthropic efforts and community support. As Greenberg Traurig continues to expand its global reach with approximately 3,100 lawyers across 51 locations, its commitment to social responsibility remains a core aspect of its identity.

+ GOODWIN PROCTER LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ ESPERION'S $1.1B TAKE-PRIVATE DEAL SEES SIDLEY AND GOODWIN AT THE HELM

In a significant move within the life sciences sector, Esperion Therapeutics Inc. has entered into a $1.1 billion take-private agreement, with prominent law firms Sidley Austin LLP and Goodwin Procter LLP spearheading the legal aspects of the transaction. This strategic acquisition is poised to reshape Esperion's operational framework, allowing the company to focus on its core competencies without the pressures associated with public market fluctuations. The transaction, announced on 1st May 2026, marks a pivotal moment for Esperion, which specializes in lipid management therapies.The deal is particularly noteworthy given the growing interest in private equity investments within the biotechnology space. By going private, Esperion aims to streamline its research and development processes and accelerate the commercialization of its innovative therapies. Law firms Sidley and Goodwin, recognized for their expertise in Mergers & Acquisitions, are expected to provide comprehensive support, ensuring a seamless transition during this transformative phase for the company. Their involvement also underscores the legal complexities that accompany such a high-stakes transaction.Esperion's decision to pursue a take-private strategy comes amid a broader trend of biotech firms seeking to enhance value through private ownership. Industry analysts suggest that this move could foster a more favorable environment for long-term strategic planning and investment in R&D. Investors are likely to monitor the performance of Esperion closely, as the company adjusts to its new status and seeks to enhance shareholder value post-transaction. The potential for accelerated growth in a less scrutinized environment presents a compelling narrative for both current and prospective investors.Sidley and Goodwin's participation in this deal illustrates their strong positioning within the private equity landscape, leveraging their extensive networks and legal acumen to facilitate complex transactions. The firms' reputations for excellence in handling high-profile deals will not only benefit Esperion but may also attract further opportunities in the life sciences sector. As the deal progresses, stakeholders will be keen to assess how the new private structure impacts Esperion's strategic initiatives and overall market performance.As Esperion embarks on this new path, the collaboration between Sidley and Goodwin in executing the $1.1 billion take-private deal will be closely watched by industry insiders and investors alike. This landmark transaction is expected to set a precedent for future deals within the biotech arena, highlighting the ongoing evolution of market dynamics in the face of advancing medical technology and shifting investor interests.

+ ORRICK WELCOMES YOUTH ONLINE SAFETY EXPERT NICOLE LOPEZ

Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe has announced the addition of Nicole Lopez, a leading authority in youth online safety policy and litigation, to its team. This strategic move comes at a crucial time as the firm aims to enhance its capabilities in navigating the evolving landscape of online safety. On 28th April 2026, Lopez expressed her enthusiasm for the new role and the opportunity to make a broader impact in the field.Lopez, who previously held a significant position at Meta, emphasized the importance of collaboration in her new role at Orrick. "I am so immensely proud of the pioneering work we did at Meta as a team, where I worked with incredibly talented colleagues who cared so deeply about getting it right," she stated. Her experience and expertise will be invaluable as Orrick seeks to provide comprehensive solutions for its clients facing increasing scrutiny over online safety issues.The urgency of youth online safety has escalated, with 25 state legislatures actively considering various forms of legislation aimed at safeguarding minors in digital environments. Jeremy Kudon, who leads Orrick’s State Legislative practice, noted, "We are facing a watershed moment. Online safety is top of mind for innovators, government, teens and parents, and society at large." Lopez's leadership in this conversation positions Orrick to effectively guide clients through the legislative maze.Aravind Swaminathan, Orrick’s Tech Sector Co-Leader, highlighted Lopez's multifaceted expertise, stating, "Nicole is part policy expert, part litigation and regulatory strategist, and part business advisor – she will be an extraordinary resource to our clients." As artificial intelligence continues to reshape the digital landscape, the need for proactive measures in youth safety is more pressing than ever, and Orrick is poised to be at the forefront of these changes.Orrick's commitment to developing robust online safety solutions is further exemplified by its Online Safety Law Center, which aims to keep clients informed of rapid developments in this area. With Nicole Lopez joining the team, the firm is well-positioned to advocate for effective policies and practices that balance innovation and child protection, ensuring that both companies and young users can thrive in a safer online environment.

urn:contentItem:6JJF-TTT3-SF9G-K02M-00000-00ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION - THE RECENT NEWS IN THE SECTOR AND ITS PEERS2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:02:20ZLexisNexisSTOCKROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION - THE RECENT NEWS IN THE SECTOR AND ITS PEERS

DASHBOARD: ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION

PermID4296528943Websitehttps://www.rockefellerfoundation.org/IndustryPhilanthropy / Global DevelopmentAddress420 Fifth Avenue, New York City, New York, U.S.

ACTIVITIES:

The Rockefeller Foundation, founded in 1913 by John D. Rockefeller, is a global philanthropy headquartered in New York City. With around 200 staff, it manages a $6 billion endowment, granting $300 million annually to advance health, equity, and sustainability. In 2024, it committed $100 million to climate resilience in African cities. Its culture emphasizes innovation, collaboration, and data-driven impact, with training in global policy and grantmaking. Competing with Ford Foundation, Rockefeller stands out for its pioneering role in public health and green energy, including founding the Global Energy Alliance for People and Planet. Recent initiatives include AI for social good and support for the National Museum of African American History, aligning with social justice trends. The foundations century-long legacy includes eradicating hookworm and funding the Green Revolution, shaping modern philanthropy.

+ ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION [3 News That Matters]

+ ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION MARKS 60 YEARS IN NAIROBI, PAVING NEW PATHS IN AFRICAN DEVELOPMENT

NAIROBI — The Rockefeller Foundation celebrated the 60th anniversary of its Africa Regional Office, established in Nairobi, Kenya, in 1966, on the 1st of May, 2026. This milestone event was part of the AfricaXchange 2026, which aims to catalyze systemic reform by uniting leaders who can deploy capital, influence policy discussions, and scale viable solutions to the continent's pressing challenges. The Rockefeller Foundation has a rich legacy of over a century of investing in human capital, health, agriculture, and energy across Africa, and this event brought together more than 400 leaders from various sectors to discuss collaborative solutions and advocate for systemic change.William Asiko, Senior Vice President and head of The Rockefeller Foundation’s Africa Regional Office, emphasized the changing landscape of development finance during his opening remarks. "African philanthropy, private capital, and regional institutions are reshaping not just how development is financed, but how it is delivered," he stated. His remarks come at a time when the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) reported a significant decline in official development assistance, highlighting the urgent need for new models of investment and partnership in Africa. The AfricaXchange event sought to position Africa not merely as a recipient of aid but as an equal partner in shaping its economic destiny.The closing session featured Elizabeth Yee, Executive Vice President of Programs at The Rockefeller Foundation, who remarked on the importance of collective action. "Philanthropy’s job is not to be the hero. It’s to take risk others can’t, build trust where it’s been missing, and move countries from ambition to execution," she noted. The conference's theme, "Money, Markets, and Mindsets," captured the ambition of ensuring that Africa's future is built, financed, and led by Africans. The Rockefeller Foundation also launched the inaugural class of Africa Big Bets Fellows, ten changemakers driving community-led solutions across several African countries.AfricaXchange 2026 served as a platform for discussions centered on activating capital and policy levers necessary for self-determination. Leaders from diverse fields tackled urgent issues such as energy transition, climate-resilient agriculture, and health systems transformation. Among the distinguished participants were E. Prof. Anyang’ Nyong’o, Governor of Kisumu County, and E. Nardos Bekele-Thomas, CEO of the African Union Development Agency. Their presence underscored the collaborative spirit needed to address the continent's challenges while fostering sustainable growth and resilience.The Rockefeller Foundation's commitment to Africa began long before the Nairobi office opened, with initiatives dating back to the early 20th century, including the establishment of the West Africa Yellow Fever Commission in 1925. Today, the Foundation dedicates approximately one-third of its funding to Africa, priori

+ ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION LAUNCHES BIG BETS FELLOWSHIP TO TRANSFORM AFRICA

The Rockefeller Foundation has launched the inaugural class of Africa Big Bets Fellows during its AfricaXchange convening in Nairobi, Kenya. The first cohort includes 10 changemakers from Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Nigeria, South Africa, and Tanzania, selected to lead innovative, community-driven solutions addressing key development challenges across the continent. The fellowship will run for five months and aims to help scale impactful ideas in areas such as energy access, food security, healthcare, financial inclusion, climate resilience, and migration.This initiative coincides with the 28th of April, 2026, marking the 60th anniversary of the Rockefeller Foundation’s Africa Regional Office and reflects its long-standing engagement in the continent’s development. Leaders from the Foundation emphasized that the fellowship supports a new generation of African innovators whose locally driven solutions are designed to expand opportunity, strengthen resilience, and improve quality of life across diverse sectors including health, agriculture, clean energy, and digital finance.Across Africa, the program comes at a time when major structural challenges persist, including limited access to electricity for hundreds of millions of people and significant gaps in global climate finance. Despite these challenges, Africa holds vast potential for renewable energy development and sustainable growth, with projections indicating that a large share of future energy demand could be met through clean energy sources if adequately supported.The selected fellows are working on a wide range of solutions, including mobile healthcare services in rural Ghana, waste-to-resource systems for cleaner cities, regenerative agriculture linked to school feeding programs in Kenya, AI-powered farming innovation in Malawi, and improved access to financial services in Nigeria. Other projects focus on clean energy access for off-grid communities, climate-resilient farming, youth-led media platforms, and real-time migration and climate information systems.The fellowship is part of a broader AfricaXchange platform that brings together funders, practitioners, and policymakers to strengthen locally led development. It reflects a growing emphasis on ensuring that African solutions are designed, financed, and led within the continent, while also connecting innovators to global networks and resources to scale their impact. The Rockefeller Foundation highlighted that this initiative builds on more than a century of work in Africa, continuing its legacy by supporting emerging leaders whose ideas aim to deliver measurable, scalable, and sustainable impact across the continent and beyond.

+ ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION INVESTS IN WORKFORCE RESILIENCE AMID AI CHALLENGES

The Rockefeller Foundation is taking a proactive stance against the impending challenges posed by artificial intelligence on the workforce, committing significant funding to a national initiative aimed at enhancing workforce resilience. On 28th April 2026, the century-old philanthropic institution announced its strategy to address the disruption caused by automation, focusing on preventing job displacement and helping workers transition into AI-complementary roles.The initiative is designed as a grant-based program that prioritizes workforce interventions over product development. The Rockefeller Foundation's approach emphasizes the importance of program design, metrics, and evaluation. Central to this effort will be the measurement of displacement risk, deployment outcomes, and labor-market transitions, which are crucial for understanding the impact of automation on employment.Funding for the initiative is expected to come through various instruments, including grants, programmatic pilots, and partnerships with employers, training providers, and local governments. The choices made during the implementation phase will be critical in determining whether the Rockefeller Foundation's funding leads to scalable reskilling pipelines or merely funds isolated projects with limited long-term impact.This move by the Rockefeller Foundation serves as a strategic philanthropic signal rather than a regulatory or corporate initiative. By investing at this scale, the foundation aims to catalyze innovative models for retraining and public-private collaboration, complementing ongoing discussions about workforce policies such as universal basic income, sectoral training, and apprenticeship programs. This initiative is poised to increase the demand for measurable, data-driven evaluations within the skills and labor analytics ecosystem.As the Rockefeller Foundation rolls out this substantial program, stakeholders will be keenly observing its funding priorities, evaluation criteria, and partnership dynamics. Key considerations will include whether the initiative emphasizes durable credentialing, employer engagement, or short-term placement metrics. Ultimately, the decisions made now will be pivotal in determining whether this initiative can effectively influence labor-market outcomes or become primarily a source of funding for pilot programs.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ FORD FOUNDATION [3 News That Matters]

+ FORD FOUNDATION UNVEILS $10M HOMETOWN FUND TO REVITALIZE ROSEBURG

ROSEBURG, Ore. – The Ford Foundation is launching the Hometown Fund, a $10 million investment aimed at revitalizing Roseburg over the next decade. The date of this announcement is 5th May 2026. This initiative is designed to support large-scale, community-driven capital projects within the city limits, with the foundation offering capital grants of up to $1 million and pre-development planning requests of up to $75,000. The Ford Foundation aims to unite partners from both the private and public sectors to create impactful transformations in the community."So I really, really hope that the grown-ups can work together to build tangible spaces and places where kids can say that they are loved and that there’s a future here," stated Kara Inae Carlisle, President and CEO of the Ford Foundation. She emphasized the importance of creating meaningful changes in the community over the next decade, reflecting a commitment to fostering environments where families can thrive. This local initiative builds upon the foundation's extensive legacy of charitable giving, which has amounted to approximately $810 million since its inception.Historically, the Ford Foundation has focused on education, early childhood development, and community economic development. Its flagship Ford Scholars Program currently supports around 1,000 students annually, providing them with the necessary resources to pursue their academic goals. The foundation has also played a crucial role in regional crisis recovery and long-term community building, such as its partnership with the Oregon Community Foundation and Meyer Memorial Trust to distribute $10 million for community rebuilding following the devastating 2020 Labor Day wildfires.Organizations interested in applying for the Hometown Fund must start by completing an online information form that outlines their project’s scope and its expected impact on the community. After the initial submission, staff from the Ford Foundation will review the proposal and reach out to schedule a meeting with the applicant. Projects that align with the fund’s objectives of fostering multi-sector collaboration and large-scale transformation will receive an invitation to complete a formal application.The Hometown Fund represents a significant commitment by the Ford Foundation to invest in the future of Roseburg, aiming to create lasting change that benefits both current residents and future generations. As the foundation seeks to unite various stakeholders across sectors, the initiative reflects a broader vision for community revitalization in rural areas.

+ FORD FOUNDATION RECOGNIZED BY NHRC FOR COMMITMENT TO HUMAN RIGHTS IN NIGERIA

The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) has honored the Ford Foundation for its sustained contributions to advancing human rights in Nigeria, reinforcing a partnership that has played a critical role in strengthening access to justice and institutional capacity. On 28th April 2026, during a ceremony in Abuja, the NHRC presented an Award of Appreciation to the Foundation in recognition of its longstanding support.The award was received by the Foundation’s Regional Director for West Africa, ChiChi Aniagolu-Okoye, during a high-level engagement with directors and senior officials of the Commission. Speaking at the event, the Executive Secretary of the NHRC, Dr. Tony Ojukwu, commended the Ford Foundation’s consistent backing, particularly in initiatives aimed at expanding access to justice and strengthening the Commission’s operational capacity.Dr. Ojukwu emphasized that given the breadth and complexity of human rights challenges in Nigeria, the Commission cannot function in isolation. He stated that sustained collaboration with development partners remains essential to transforming human rights protection from policy intentions into tangible outcomes for citizens.In her remarks, Aniagolu-Okoye described the recognition as both significant and encouraging, reaffirming the Foundation’s commitment to promoting human rights. She noted that a key focus of the Foundation’s work is ensuring that citizens are not only aware of their rights but are also supported in seeking justice when those rights are violated. However, she emphasized that awareness alone is insufficient without strong and effective enforcement systems.The engagement underscored the importance of continued cooperation between the Commission and its partners in addressing evolving human rights challenges across the country. In a vote of thanks, Senior Human Rights Adviser to the Commission, Hilary Ogbonna, expressed appreciation for the Ford Foundation’s enduring partnership, highlighting key institutional advancements, including initiatives such as the MOVE project and the Commission’s dashboard system, which benefitted from the Foundation’s early support.

+ FORD FOUNDATION URGES ECOWAS FIRST LADIES TO ADDRESS GENDER-BASED VIOLENCE

The Ford Foundation has called on First Ladies across the ECOWAS region to deploy their influence more strategically in tackling gender-based violence (GBV). The Foundation described the First Ladies’ 'soft power' as a critical driver of social change and institutional accountability. This call was made by ChiChi Aniagolu, West Africa Regional Director of the Ford Foundation, while delivering a goodwill message at the ECOWAS First Ladies’ Forum on Zero Tolerance for Gender-Based Violence, held in Banjul under the SWEDD+ platform in partnership with the World Bank and the United Nations Population Fund on 24th April 2026.Aniagolu warned that despite existing frameworks and commitments, gender-based violence remains widespread, with one in three women globally affected. In West and Central Africa, she noted, harmful practices such as child marriage persist, with over 30 percent of girls in some countries married before the age of 18. She also highlighted the economic burden of GBV, estimating losses of up to 3.7 percent of Gross Domestic Product in affected countries — figures that exceed investments in key sectors such as education.While the Ford Foundation acknowledged that First Ladies are not formal policymakers, it stressed their significant role in shaping public discourse and influencing decision-making across the continent. Aniagolu cited examples of impactful First Ladies, including Maryam Babangida, whose Better Life for Rural Women Programme elevated rural women's issues into national discussions, and Fatima Bio, whose 'Hands Off Our Girls' campaign contributed to a national emergency declaration on rape in 2019. Such instances illustrate how 'soft power,' when utilized effectively, can instigate systemic change.The Ford Foundation has continued to invest in strengthening GBV prevention and response systems across West Africa, including partnerships involving First Ladies and governors’ spouses in Nigeria. These interventions have aimed to advance advocacy efforts, improve evidence-based response systems, and address harmful social norms. Aniagolu emphasized that laws and policies are insufficient without public trust and accessibility, pointing out the persistent silence among survivors driven by stigma, fear, and weak institutional confidence.In light of recent regional initiatives such as the GBV Roadmap (2026–2028), Legal Scorecard, and Joint Communiqu , Aniagolu urged First Ladies to move beyond mere commitments by promoting safe reporting, challenging harmful norms, and strengthening accountability systems. 'No girl should grow up believing silence is her only option, and no woman should be denied justice because of where she lives,' she asserted, reaffirming the Ford Foundation’s commitment to supporting advocacy, strengthening systems, and amplifying women’s voices across the region.

urn:contentItem:6JJ7-HFH3-RVP0-23PB-00000-00Hochul ignores reality on climate efforts2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:02:20ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. D1Hochul ignores reality on climate effortsByMark DunleaSpecial to the USA TODAY Network

Solar is the cheapest source of electricity on the planet, though government red tape and bureaucracy in the U.S. has slowed down adoption compared to other countries such as those in Europe.

In a recent op-ed published by Empire Report - "Advocates opposing Climate Act changes ignoring reality" - Ken Lovett, a communications advisor to Gov. Kathy Hochul, continues the pattern of misleading and incomplete information promoted by her administration to defend her efforts to avoid complying with the Climate Leadership and Community Protection Act, or CLCPA.

While Lovett and Hochul defend her retreat on the grounds that the world has changed since the CLCPA was passed, they ignore that the biggest change is that global warming and extreme weather are continuing to accelerate, even faster than expected. Former President Joe Biden and Sen. Bernie Sanders of Vermont have repeatedly warned that climate change presents an existential threat to humanity. The secretary-general of the United Nations has repeatedly warned that the slow climate efforts by governments have opened up the gates to Hell.

New York's climate policy should be driven by science

Climate action should be a matter of science, not politics and election spin. Even the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, or IPCC, admits that its climate emission reduction goals - faster than the CLCPA - are too slow to avoid catastrophic climate change. To avoid that fate, the IPCC promotes carbon capture, even while admitting that it is not presently feasible. Climate activist Greta Thunberg chided them in her address to the UN for leaving the fate of her generation to the success of a Hail Mary pass.

Converting the state's energy system from fossil fuels to clean renewable energy certainly poses challenges. The biggest challenge, however, is not technical but political. Meeting timelines requires coordinated planning and strong leadership at the top - both of which have been missing under Hochul.

While President Donald Trump certainly deserves blame for his war against clean renewable energy and his promotion of the fossil fuel industry and their high prices, New York's failure to meet renewable energy goals stretches back decades, long before Trump. Gov. George Pataki, for instance, set goals in 2002 for renewable energy which the state's failed to come close to meeting.

The CLCPA, adopted in 2019, largely put into statue climate policies that had been official state policy since a 2009 gubernatorial executive order. Two hundred climate and community groups had supported a stronger bill that would have required a much stronger planning process while updating the state's climate goals, including making them more aligned with the lower heating target developing nations had won in the Paris climate accords. It also had a much faster timeline with short-term benchmarks.

To get legislators and the executive to act, you need 2-year goals and benchmarks, not 10-to-30-year milestones.

New York moves as slow as molasses. After the climate law passed, the state spent three years to develop a scoping plan for climate action. Even though the plan was largely written by the governor's office, Hochul has ignored many of its recommendations, including ignoring the legal mandate to incorporate it into the recent update to the state energy master plan.

The state's climate scoping plan provided a low-ball estimate of needing $10billion annually for 30 years to fund the transition to a renewable energy future. Lovett wants us to applaud Hochul for providing $1billion - to be spent over five years - in last year's budget for a Sustainable Future Fund, which represents 2% of what her administration previously said was needed.

Both plans were more of a college white paper, outlining key questions and visions rather than providing a detailed plan of action with budget, timelines, and intermediate goals. The governor, for instance, rammed in support for extremely expensive nuclear power plants contrary to the scoping plan. New York continues to provide more money to nuclear subsidies than it does to renewables, with Hochul recently adding $30billion plus over two decades to the initiative started by Cuomo to keep four unprofitable, old, upstate nuclear reactors open that the owner wanted to close. Lawmakers should impose a two-year moratorium on nuclear to do a financial assessment while redirecting the $30 billion to renewable energy.

Another example of slow action is the issue of the state power plant that powers the Capitol and the Empire State Plaza in Albany. For more than a century, the plant has polluted Sheridan Hollow, a low-income community of color, by burning coal, oil, trash and now gas. Since 2017, climate and community groups have been calling to shut down the plant and use geothermal instead. The state finally did a study, which took two-plus years to complete and then proposed a 15-to-20-year timeline. Meanwhile, the state of Michigan took 18 months - from study to completion - to convert its state capitol to geothermal.

A second study of the New York Capitol complex was done as part of a budget agreement to look at how to decarbonize the state's 15 largest greenhouse gas emitters. Lawmakers had initially sought a concrete commitment to do the conversions. That two-year study was finally completed in January, but NYPA says it can't release it until June- after the post-legislative session - since it argues it has to redact details about individual buildings.

Hochul recently stated that it would be foolish for anyone - including her and her staff - to imply that high utility bills in New York are due to the climate law giving the slowness of her administration in implementing it. States with lower electric costs also tend to be the ones with a higher percentage of their electricity coming from renewable energy.

As even the New York Independent System Operator has acknowledged, the main driver of high energy costs is reliance on natural gas and its volatile prices, a situation made worse by the present Trump-Netanyahu war on Iran. Continued investment in expensive gas pipelines also drives up costs. Hochul recently had the state reverse course and approve the Northeast Supply Enanchement gas pipeline under New York Harbor.

Solar is the cheapest source of electricity on the planet, though government red tape and bureaucracy in the Unted States has slowed down adoption compared to other countries such as those in Europe. In Bill McKibben's new book, "Here Comes the Sun," he points out that everyday Pakistanis - with virtually no help from their government - have installed a massive number of cheap solar units from neighboring China to escape from the high cost and unreliability of the existing electric grid. In only six years, solar has gone from 0 to 30% of Pakistan's energy supply. Texas makes it easier than New York to build renewable energy and leads the country in the development of solar, wind, and geothermal. California often meets 100% of its energy needs from renewables and battery storage for at least part of the day.

Besides more funding for solar, lawmakers should pass several bills - ASAP, SUNNY - to speed up solar development..

Climate change is a public health crisis in New York

Lovett and Hochul ignore that massive costs and negative health impacts that New Yorkers are already experiencing from climate change. New Yorkers annually pay an estimated $50 billion in Increased health care costs due to air pollution. Flooding is an increasing problem, especially in New York City, with some even dying in basement apartments. Air quality has plummeted in recent summers due to wildfires in Canada. Extreme heat is the leading cause of weather-related deaths in the U.S and New York state, with more than 500 residents of New York City alone dying annually.

One way to achieve lower energy costs is through public power, as has been done in many states - though public control is also critical. Even in New York, we have seen this with the 50-plus municipally owned utilities plus the cheap hydropower from the New York Power Authority. Yet Hochul and the NYPA are resisting the direction by the state Legislature to build publicly owned renewables.

Lovett also trots out the much maligned - and indeed fraudulent - 3-page memo by NYSERDA that contends that the Governor's cap-and-trade/invest proposal for carbon pricing "would cost upstate households more than $4,000 annually on their utility bills." In order to come up with the $4,000 estimate, Hochul is using a price that is more than 800% higher than what she has been proposing, a fact not mentioned in the NYSERDA paper.

Carbon pricing - making fossil fuels pay for the damages their use causes to the rest of society - is viewed by economists as the single most effective way to reduce their burning and convert to renewable energy. While a price on carbon is intended to raise the price of fossil fuels in order to reduce the use, rebating part or all of the revenue reduces or eliminates the negative impact on people's wallets. Hochul has proposed a 33% rebate. A carbon tax bill that I drafted a decade ago along with some economic professors had a rebate of 60%, targeted to low- and middle-income New Yorkers which would have held them harmless. Some groups prefer a 100% rebate. (And perhaps the only positive thing from COVID-19 is that the state now knows how to send checks to individual New Yorkers without relying on tax filings to do that.)

Most climate groups prefer a carbon tax approach instead. Environmental justice - and even the late Pope Francis - have long opposed the cap-and-trade approach promoted by Hochul as hurting such communities while being subject to Wall Street speculation. Interestingly, both fossil fuel companies and senior Republicans - not Trump - have long supported a carbon tax, though they want it to be national for standardization and with the poison bill of absolving them of any legal liability for knowingly causing climate change.

The state's climate law does not require a price on carbon. It does require a plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, a deadline which Hochul missed. Hochul chose cap-and-trade to do this. One problem with this approach is that in the last moments before the plan is formally adopted, politicians invariably weaken the targets to appease the fossil fuel industry. In this case, Hochul is seeking to weaken it much earlier in the process.

In 2010, the Green Party first proposed a Green New Deal, which combined a 10-year timeline to 100% renewables and zero emissions with an economic bill of rights that inlcuded living-wage jobs, universal health care, and education. A key point is that we need a just transition, that guarantees a decent standard of living for everyone.

With Trump installed as climate-denier-in-chief, it is critical that state governments step up to take effective climate action. Hochul unfortunately is not the only Democratic governor that is responding to high energy prices - caused by excessive profits and exploitation of the fossil fuel industry and utilities - by backing away from existing climate goals. Fortunately, many state lawmakers, especially from downstate, have been resisting so far. For the sake of our planet, our grandchildren, and ourselves, state lawmakers need to say no to Hochul and redouble efforts to speed up climate action.

Mark Dunlea is author of "Putting Out the Planetary Fire."

Solar is the cheapest source of electricity on the planet, though government red tape and bureaucracy in the U.S. has slowed down adoption compared to other countries such as those in Europe.
